interrogator_dossier:
# Forensic Dossier — thyssenkrupp AG (TKA.DE)

*Interrogator's report to the Investment Committee · transcripts FY24/25 Q1 (2025-02-13) → FY25/26 Q1 (2026-02-12) · cross-referenced against screener financials and live corrections as of 2026-03-31.*

---

## 1. NARRATIVE ARC

The story across these six quarters is **not** an operating turnaround — it is a **disassembly**. Management's language migrates steadily away from "we will fix the businesses" and toward "we will break the company into independently listed parts and let the market value them." The through-line is the **ACES 2030 holding-company conversion**.

- **Q1 24/25 (Feb-2025):** The frame is still *operational resilience + portfolio tidying*. The two named portfolio items are a **Marine Systems minority spin** ("finalize in the course of calendar year 2025") and a **Steel Europe 50/50 JV with EPCG**. APEX (cost program) is "2.0," restructuring is "necessary but paves the way." Green-transformation proof-points (CO2 steel for VW, Polysius carbon capture, nucera electrolyzers) are floated as future demand. Net cash is the recurring anchor: "€4.3 billion net cash."
- **Q2 (May-2025):** The Steel narrative hardens — "agreement in principle with IG Metall," AT cost-cut of "€150 million" plus "phaseout of the production site in Hagen." Marine momentum builds (Singapore submarine extension). The group "repaid our last bond… makes the group debt-free."
- **Q3 (Aug-2025):** The pivot is now explicit and structural: thyssenkrupp as "a **strategic holding company with independently managed business segments**… unlock and crystallize value." New CFO Axel Hamann arrives. AT is split "into 4 business units to sharpen our capital market readiness." Steel restructuring agreement "reached consensus this July."
- **Q4 / FY (Dec-2025):** **ACES 2030** is formally branded; "year of decisions" → "**year of execution**." TKMS spin **completed and listed** (Oct-20, MDAX inclusion, "over 14% value creation… on top of the preceding tkAG share price increase of approximately 240%"). Steel pivots from JV to an outright **sale to Jindal** ("majority"). HKM exit agreed with Salzgitter; slab supply to TK Steel "will already end in 2028."
- **Q1 25/26 (Feb-2026):** Materials Services pushed "for capital market readiness and their respective stand-alone setup"; Automation Engineering put up for sale; Jindal due diligence "on its way." The new tailwind introduced: **EU steel policy** (CBAM, safeguard tariffs) — "they do present upside potential."

**Growth drivers quietly dropped:** the green-transformation / hydrogen (nucera) story, front-and-center in 2025, has faded to background — by Q1 25/26 Decarbon Technologies sales are *−19%* with nucera electrolyzer weakness explicitly called out. The thesis has **contracted from "green industrial champion" to "holdco that monetizes hidden assets (TKMS, TKE, Materials) and amputates Steel."**

## 2. CLAIMS vs FINANCIALS (forensic)

| Management claim | Quarter | The numbers | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| "Most KPIs above prior year… solid quarter" | Q1 24/25 | FY25 revenue €32.84B (−6.3% YoY, −7.2% 3-yr CAGR); net income €465M but on €800M impairments + one-off elevator/India gains | **Poorer than narrated.** Top line is in structural decline; the "stable EBIT-adjusted" is real but achieved by amputating volume, not winning it. |
| "Third consecutive year of positive free cash flow before M&A… first time in 20 years" | recurring | FY25 FCF-before-M&A +€363M — **but** this is *before M&A* and includes a Marine **prepayment** pulled into Q1; the screener's TTM FCF is **−€926M** and P/FCF is a meaningless 53x | **Half-true / engineered.** "Positive FCF" leans on advance customer payments (MS) and a generous definition; unlevered cash generation is weak (FCF margin 0.4%). |
| "Strong, largely unchanged €4.3B net cash… makes the group debt-free" | Q1–Q2 24/25 | Live recompute 2026-03-31: **NET CASH €2.74B** (cash €3.55B vs debt €806M). Net cash fell from ~€5B (Sep-25) to €3.2B (Dec-25) to €2.74B as restructuring cash-out and seasonal NWC bit | **True but eroding.** The balance sheet is the genuine crown jewel and a real SoP add-back, but the cash pile is **shrinking** as restructuring is funded. |
| "We keep our profit guidance" (repeated 6x) | every quarter | EBIT-adjusted FY25 €640M, +€72M YoY — **held flat against a €2.2B revenue drop**. Beat rate only **1 of 6 quarters (17%)** | **Operationally credible on EBIT, weak on delivery.** They defend the profit line via cost-out, but the broader guidance/delivery record is poor (17% beat rate; EPS 3-yr CAGR −25.6%). |
| Steel "industrial future concept… resilience" | Q3–Q4 | ~€600M of the €800M FY25 impairments are **Steel Europe**; ROE −4.1%, ROIC −2.0%; Altman-Z 1.8 (distress zone) | **The narrative is far richer than the asset.** Steel is value-destructive; management's own serial impairments confirm it. |
| "Over 14% value creation" from TKMS spin | Q4 | Real and verifiable — TKMS now ~€5.36B market cap, MDAX member; TKA's retained 51% ≈ €2.7B | **Corroborated.** The one place where the crystallization thesis has actually delivered cash-equivalent value to shareholders. |

**Net:** the EBIT-resilience claim survives scrutiny; the FCF and "company is healthy" claims do **not**. The real value is **balance-sheet + listed-stub** value (net cash, TKMS, TKE, soon Materials), **not** earnings power.

## 3. TONE & CONFIDENCE TRAJECTORY — **PIVOTING (defensive on ops, offensive on portfolio)**

- **Q1 24/25:** Cautiously offensive — "record-breaking new orders at Marine," guidance *raised* on FCF. *"Quarter 1 figures look promising to us."*
- **Q2:** More defensive — *"markets don't help… many industries remain muted"*; sales guidance lowered, profit held. LTI accrual hit from the share-price run-up is a tell of how far the stock had already moved.
- **Q3:** New CFO, careful and hedged — EBIT guidance "specified… to the lower end"; net income −€255M with a €150M deferred-tax write-off from the spin mechanics.
- **Q4:** Confident on **portfolio** ("year of execution," TKMS done), guarded on **steel** ("cannot be excluded" re further impairments — said three separate times across Q3/Q4/Q1).
- **Q1 25/26:** Markedly more upbeat on the **external** lever — *"the sentiment has turned… increases in the share prices of around 50%… clear positive sentiment"* on EU steel policy — but this is borrowed confidence from a **sector-wide** re-rating, not company execution.

The shift from offensive (growth/green) to defensive (efficiency/cost/macro-policy) language is unmistakable. Confidence is **rising on the breakup mechanics and falling on the underlying industrial demand.**

## 4. GUIDANCE CREDIBILITY — **Medium**

They have done what they said on **two things that matter**: (a) delivered three straight years of positive FCF-before-M&A as promised, and (b) executed the TKMS spin on the stated calendar-2025 timetable. That is a real, checkable track record of doing the portfolio things they commit to.

But the **earnings beat rate is 17% (1/6)**, EPS 3-yr CAGR is −25.6%, and "we keep profit guidance" was only achievable by repeatedly **lowering the sales** assumption underneath it. Steel impairments recur every quarter despite "this is hopefully the last one" (said in Q2 and again implied later). The guidance is **directionally honest but consistently set at a level the top line keeps undercutting** — credible on portfolio milestones, soft on operating delivery. **Reliability: Medium.**

## 5. ANALYST PRESSURE POINTS

Analysts (Synagowitz of Deutsche Bank, Fairclough, Obst, Gabriel) circle the **same four unanswered questions** call after call:

1. **"Net cash €4B+, market cap €3B — you could buy the whole company back"** (Fairclough, Q1 24/25). Lopez deflects: *"We are not doing this theoretical analysis right now."* The SoP discount is so glaring that analysts state it to management's face; management refuses to engage.
2. **How much cash gets trapped/seeded into each spun entity?** Repeatedly pushed (Synagowitz Q3: "is there a floor you could guide us towards"). Management **will not disclose** the starting balance sheet of the spun businesses — a recurring non-answer that matters enormously for the residual TKA stub value.
3. **Marine guarantees / freed capital** (Fairclough Q1): management concedes the guarantees "**do not immediately free up capital**" — puncturing a bull talking point.
4. **Further Steel impairments?** Asked every quarter; answer is always "cannot be excluded." This is the topic that has **repeatedly turned into bad news** (the €600M FY25 steel impairment).

What analysts worry about that management isn't addressing: **the residual stub.** As TKMS, then Materials, then potentially TKE and Steel leave, what is left in TKA AG, what net cash stays vs. seeds the spins, and what the holding-company conglomerate discount on the remaining pieces will be.

## 6. RED FLAGS / GREEN FLAGS

🔴 **Steel is a serial value-destroyer** — €600M+ of FY25's €800M impairments; ROIC −2%, "cannot be excluded" re further write-downs (Q1 25/26).
🔴 **TTM FCF is −€926M** (live) despite the "3 years positive FCF" headline — the cash story depends on Marine prepayments and a before-M&A definition.
🔴 **Net cash is melting** — €5B (Sep-25) → €3.2B (Dec-25) → €2.74B (Mar-26) as restructuring (€700–800M guided FY25/26) is funded out of the balance sheet.
🔴 **Revenue in secular decline** — −6% to −8% YoY every quarter; the conglomerate is shrinking, not stabilizing.
🔴 **Steel slab self-supply ends 2028** (HKM exit) — a structural input-cost cliff for the steel stub.
🔴 **17% earnings beat rate** and EPS 3-yr CAGR −25.6%.

🟢 **TKMS spin executed and value-accretive** — completed Oct-2025, MDAX, "14% value creation"; the retained 51% ≈ €2.7B is real, listed, mark-to-market value.
🟢 **Genuine net-cash balance sheet** (€2.74B) and **group is bond-debt-free** — a real SoP add-back, rare for a distressed industrial.
🟢 **Materials Services / tk accelis is the next dated crystallization** — capital-market-readiness push; a clean, profitable (€11.4B sales, €50M+ EBIT-adj/qtr) trading franchise.
🟢 **EBIT-adjusted held flat through a €2.2B revenue drop** — cost discipline (APEX) is real; 4,800 FTE out in FY25.
🟢 **TKE (Elevator) stake** booked ~€2B with optionality ("talk in the market around IPO").

## 7. HIDDEN SIGNALS

- **Terminology migration:** "portfolio optimization" (early) → "**strategic holding company**" (Q3) → "**lean financial holding company**" / "ACES 2030" (Q4–Q1 25/26). The endpoint became explicit only late: TKA wants to be a **financial holding** owning stakes, not an operator. That reframes the correct valuation lens from earnings to **sum-of-the-stubs minus stub holdco discount**.
- **The Steel exit route flipped** — from "50/50 JV with EPCG" (Q1 24/25) to "sale to Jindal, majority" (Q4). [POST-TRANSCRIPT: this has since flipped *again* — see §8 / catalyst verification.] The instability of the Steel disposal path across four quarters is itself a credibility signal: they do not control this outcome.
- **New CFO Hamann** is more numerate and more hedged than predecessor Schulte — more willing to say "cannot be excluded," "too early to tell," "we won't provide further detail." Less salesmanship, more risk-flagging.
- **Capital allocation is conspicuously NOT a buyback** despite analysts repeatedly pointing out the company trades below net cash + stakes. Management's refusal ("not within the next 24 months") signals cash is **earmarked for restructuring and spin-seeding**, not shareholder returns — confirming the stub-dilution worry.

## 8. CAPITAL-ALLOCATION VERDICT (for the director)

**Classification: PIVOTING.** This is a **special-situation breakup / holdco-conversion**, not an operating turnaround. The correct way to underwrite TKA is sum-of-parts (net cash + TKMS 51% + TKE stake + Materials/tk accelis + a near-zero-or-negative option on Steel), **minus** the cash that seeds each spin and the residual-stub conglomerate discount — **not** a P/E or P/FCF on the consolidated entity (where TTM FCF is negative and ROIC is −2%).

**The single most important thing the director must weigh before sizing:** *how much of the visible asset value actually accrues to the TKA stub vs. leaks away.* Every spin is a **minority (49%) distribution** that hands value directly to existing TKA holders (good) but also **consolidates a shrinking, cash-seeded residual** at the parent. The net-cash crown jewel is **melting** (€5B→€2.74B in two quarters) into restructuring. And the one catalyst that would have crystallized the worst asset — the **Jindal Steel sale** — is exactly the kind of serially-reshaped, third-party-dependent process that has flipped twice and may not close. Underwrite the **parts you can mark today** (net cash, listed TKMS, TKE) and treat Steel and any not-yet-fired spin as **option value, not base value.**

**ECONOMIC MOAT:** **NONE / ERODING.** thyssenkrupp's consolidated returns are negative (ROIC −2.0%, ROE −4.1%), gross margin 11.3% is thin for a capital-goods+steel mix, and the moat is whatever **scale-cost** advantage the individual franchises (Materials trading, TKMS submarines) carry — *not* the conglomerate. The submarine business (TKMS) genuinely has a **regulatory-license / sovereign-customer** moat, but it has already been spun out and is no longer the value driver inside TKA's *operating* base. The steel business has **no moat** — it is a structurally disadvantaged, sub-scale, high-cost European integrated producer that earns below its cost of capital and is being given away.

**SECULAR THREAT — material (not terminal for the holdco):** European integrated steel faces **energy-transition / decarbonization cost displacement** (green-steel capex with no return, carbon costs, Chinese/Asian overcapacity) — a structural impairment to the *Steel* stub's terminal value. *But* the holding-company value is dominated by **non-steel** parts (net cash, defense, elevators, materials trading) that do **not** share that tail. So the secular force is **material to one amputated limb, manageable at the holdco level.** The cheapness here is **NOT** a melting-base value trap *if* you underwrite the stubs and assign Steel ~zero; it **becomes** a value trap if you credit a consolidated earnings recovery (which the financials do not support).

---
CREDIBILITY_SCORE: 3 | TRAJECTORY: PIVOTING | MOAT: ERODING | MOAT_TREND: ERODING | SECULAR_THREAT: material

bull_thesis: thyssenkrupp is a credible, in-progress breakup of a net-cash industrial conglomerate into what management now calls a financial holding company (a parent that mainly owns and monetizes stakes in its businesses rather than running them day to day), under a plan it labels ACES 2030, and it is led by a deal team with a proven track record of getting these transactions done. The proof of concept already worked: the spin-off of the marine business TKMS (49% of the shares distributed to holders, 51% retained, listed in Frankfurt in October 2025 and added to the MDAX mid-cap index within weeks) created over 14% in value on day one, on top of a roughly 240% rise in the shares during fiscal 2024/25, and management explicitly calls it the blueprint for the other segments. The pipeline behind it is dated and real. First, the spin-off of tk accelis (the Materials Services trading arm, about EUR11.4bn in revenue) was approved by the supervisory board on 2026-06-16, with a shareholder meeting set for 2026-08-07 and a Frankfurt listing targeted before the end of 2026. Second, the combination of Kone and TK Elevator, announced 2026-04-29, monetizes thyssenkrupp's roughly 16.2% stake in TK Elevator (carried on the books at about EUR2.0bn) for around EUR3.27bn, made up of EUR810m in cash plus up to EUR2.46bn in Kone shares. Third, the divestment of Automation Engineering and a realignment of the auto unit into four business units shed loss-makers. Underneath all this, the balance sheet is a genuine floor under the value: net cash of EUR3.03bn at 2026-03-31 (cash of EUR3.84bn against total debt of EUR0.81bn), which is both a real add-back when you value the company by its parts and a cushion on the downside. Cost execution is the one operating fact the numbers back up: the APEX 2.0 program held adjusted operating profit (EBIT) flat at about EUR640m in fiscal 2025, up EUR72m year over year, even as revenue fell EUR2.2bn and headcount dropped by 4,800. And a new outside tailwind appeared in February 2026: EU steel policy (a tighter carbon border levy, import quotas, and a delay to emissions-trading costs) re-rated European steel stocks by about 50% in four months, turning the steel unit from a business it had to sell into a viable standalone, which is precisely why thyssenkrupp could afford to walk away from a lowball bid from Jindal. What has to go right: the spin-offs must list on schedule, the Kone deal must clear competition review, and the value of the listed stakes (51% of TKMS, 16% of TK Elevator, and the nucera hydrogen-tech holding) plus net cash must be worth more than the EUR6.5bn market value.
bear_thesis: The cheap entry point has already played out, and that is the central, decisive fact. The bargain the screener originally flagged was born in the February 2025 earnings call, when an analyst pointed out that net cash topped EUR4bn against a market value of EUR3bn, meaning you could effectively buy the whole company back for less than its cash. After a roughly 240% run in fiscal 2024/25, the market value is now EUR6.5bn against about EUR3.0bn of net cash: the company now trades well above its cash, the headline catalyst (the TKMS spin-off) is spent, and the gap between the share price and the value of the parts that defined the trade has largely closed. What remains underneath is a loss-making operating core: return on equity of -4.1%, return on invested capital of -2.0%, a net margin of 1.4%, a gross margin of 11.3%, revenue in a structural decline of -6% to -7% a year for three straight years, and recurring steel write-downs of about EUR600m a year against EUR2.4bn of steel pension obligations and a EUR700-800m restructuring bill. Every upbeat fiscal 2025 headline is a one-off: the three years of positive free cash flow lean on a Marine prepayment of more than EUR1bn (trailing-twelve-month free cash flow is actually -EUR926m, and the first quarter of fiscal 2025/26 alone was -EUR1.5bn); the single positive net-income year (+EUR465m) came from a +EUR902m revaluation of the Elevator stake plus the sale of the India steel business, which together washed out about EUR800m of write-downs. The remaining catalysts are weaker than the one that already fired: tk accelis is a minority (49%) spin-off, so thyssenkrupp keeps control and still consolidates it, which makes it cosmetic value-crystallization rather than a clean break-up; the Kone/TK Elevator deal is real but slow (12-18 months, with competition risk in an already-concentrated market, and about 75% of the payment coming in Kone stock rather than cash). The Jindal steel sale, a load-bearing catalyst in the earnings transcripts, was paused on 2026-05-02, so the cleanest break-up leg is dead. The operating businesses left in the parent (steel, combustion-engine-exposed auto components, low-margin materials trading, and a loss-making decarbonization unit) are the lower-quality leftovers after the crown jewel, marine, was spun out. What would confirm the bearish case: a competition-review extension or required remedies on the Kone deal, a soft tk accelis listing, the next steel write-down, or the seasonal first-half cash burn reminding the market that the operating core consumes cash.
consensus_delta: For a deep-value name the real question is whether the market is pricing permanent decline into a business whose earnings power is actually stable or growing. Here the situation is the opposite: the market has already re-rated the stock about 240% in fiscal 2024/25 and another roughly 50% on the EU steel-policy news, so the consensus is now pricing the breakup to keep delivering, not pricing terminal decline. The market assumption most exposed is that the gap between the share price and the value of the parts is still wide. It is not. At a EUR6.5bn market value, the market is paying above net cash (EUR3.0bn) for the optionality in the listed stakes (51% of TKMS plus about 16% of TK Elevator), and then some positive value on top of that for a loss-making operating core (return on invested capital of -2.0%, trailing free cash flow of -EUR926m). The genuine consensus error runs the other way from the screener's label: the buy-it-below-its-cash framing is about 18 months stale. The one place the market could still be wrong is in treating fiscal 2025's positive free cash flow and positive net income as a run-rate, when both were flattered by one-offs (the Marine prepayment; the +EUR902m Elevator revaluation), but that is a reason to be more cautious, not less. No asymmetric mispricing remains on the buy side.
forcing_function: The catalysts that drive a re-rating (the events that force the market to re-value the stock) are largely behind us or already known. The TKMS spin-off completed on 2026-10-20. The tk accelis spin-off was approved on 2026-06-16, with a shareholder meeting on 2026-08-07 and a listing before the end of 2026, but it is a minority spin-off that stays consolidated, so it crystallizes little value the market has not already discounted. The Kone/TK Elevator close is 12-18 months away (announced 2026-04-29) and depends on competition clearance. The Jindal steel sale, the one catalyst from the transcripts that could have been a clean unlock, was paused on 2026-05-02. There is no dated, binding event in the near-term window that would shatter the current consensus to the upside; the market has already absorbed the breakup. The next prints that could genuinely move consensus skew negative: seasonal cash burn, the next steel write-down, and the trading multiple tk accelis fetches on debut.
valley_of_death: These are the concrete near-term drags over the risky window of the next three to nine months. First, seasonal cash burn: the opening quarter of each fiscal year is deeply negative (the first quarter of fiscal 2025/26 was -EUR1.5bn) on bonus, working-capital, and restructuring outflows; net cash has already fallen from EUR5bn to EUR3.2bn to EUR3.0bn as one-off inflows (the India sale, the Marine prepayment) fade, and the next prints will remind the market the operating core consumes cash. Second, restructuring cash outflows escalating to EUR700-800m, the bulk of it in steel, plus HKM and auto provisions. Third, the next steel write-down: management refuses to rule one out (it says it 'cannot be excluded') every quarter, and the run-rate has been about EUR600m a year. Fourth, the tk accelis shareholder meeting (2026-08-07) and a listing before year-end risk a soft debut at a low-margin-trading multiple. Fifth, competition review on Kone/TK Elevator: a 12-18 month close in an already-concentrated elevator market invites remedies or delay, and about 75% of thyssenkrupp's payment is in Kone stock, exposed to Kone's own share price. None of these threatens the company's solvency, given the net-cash balance sheet, but each is a headwind to a re-rating before any further value is crystallized.
positioning_washout: There is limited scope for forced selling, and what exists works against owning the stock. thyssenkrupp pays only a token dividend, so there is no income-focused holder base that would be flushed out by a cut. The relevant flow is the opposite risk: after the roughly 240% run and the MDAX-index prominence of the spun-out TKMS, the names attracting passive-index and momentum money are the spin-offs (TKMS, now in the MDAX, and soon tk accelis), while the parent thyssenkrupp AG becomes the lower-quality leftover that momentum and event-driven funds rotate out of once each spin-off completes (the classic pattern of a parent underperforming after a spin-off). The 1-for-20 TKMS distribution already put marine shares directly into thyssenkrupp holders' hands, and a 49% tk accelis distribution will do the same, so holders who wanted the operating exposure get the spin-off and can sell the leftover parent. The net effect is a mild headwind to the parent, not the kind of forced-selling flush that sets up a buying opportunity.
moderator_conclusion: Our recommendation is to pass for now, with low conviction (a 2 on our internal scale). This is a textbook case of a deep-value signal that has already paid off and is being mistaken for a fresh entry. The forensic reviewer is right that management executes the breakup credibly (TKMS spun off on time, the India business sold, the bond repaid, the APEX program holding operating profit flat on a shrinking top line), but the underlying numbers are damning for a new buyer: return on invested capital of -2.0%, return on equity of -4.1%, revenue down -6% to -7% a year for three years, trailing free cash flow of -EUR926m, and every positive fiscal 2025 metric a disposal, revaluation, or prepayment one-off. Crucially, the catalyst that defined the thesis has already fired: the early-2025 arbitrage of net cash at EUR4bn against a market value of EUR3bn is gone after a roughly 240% run; the stock now trades at a EUR6.5bn market value, above its roughly EUR3.0bn of net cash, paying positive value for a loss-making operating remainder. Our reconciled sum-of-the-parts fair value (valuing each business line separately) is about EUR12.50 against a EUR10.50 share price, only about 19% upside, against an asset floor of roughly EUR8.5-9.0, which is close to a one-to-one payoff, no longer the wide asymmetry the screener implies. Under the current investing regime (our Catalyst Watch framework, June 2026), which penalizes catalysts that have already fired or that the market has already absorbed and rewards catalysts whose outcome does not depend on a single shared factor, the load-bearing remaining lever (the EU steel re-rating) is a sector-wide policy move, not company-specific edge, while the marquee unlock (TKMS) is spent and the Jindal leg is dead (paused 2026-05-02). The regime favors distressed or debt-reducing situations over spin-offs over forced sellers; thyssenkrupp offers only a minority spin-off that stays consolidated plus a slow, stock-heavy Elevator deal, which sits at the weaker end of the favored category. In short, the value is real but roughly fully priced, the operating core earns less than its cost of capital and is exposed to the energy transition, and the easy gains have been harvested. Judged purely on valuation against the roughly EUR12.50 fair value and on forensic quality (setting aside catalysts and regime), the name still only offers about 19% to a fair value that itself leans on the marks of its listed stakes, sitting atop a business with no durable competitive advantage and one that is eroding, with negative returns; the energy-transition-loser tail plus the eroding advantage cap this judgment at a 3, and the loss-making, cash-burning base pulls it to a 2. A low multiple on a structurally shrinking, below-cost-of-capital business backstopped by cash the market already credits is not value, it is a value trap with a cushion. This is a watchlist name only if the parent stub sells off after the tk accelis spin-off to a clear discount to its net cash plus listed stakes; it is not a buy here.
sop_breakdown: Our base case is about EUR12.50 a share, against a EUR10.50 share price, using a sum-of-the-parts approach (valuing each business line separately). The build: net cash of EUR3.0bn (EUR4.8 a share) plus the 51% TKMS stake at about EUR4.2bn (EUR6.7) plus the roughly 16.2% TK Elevator stake at a deal-probability-weighted EUR2.6bn (EUR4.2) plus Steel Europe as a standalone, net of pension and restructuring, at about EUR0.3bn (EUR0.5) plus Materials Services / tk accelis at about EUR1.1bn (EUR1.8) plus the auto unit at about EUR0.9bn (EUR1.4) plus the decarbonization unit at about EUR0.6bn (EUR1.0), which sums to roughly EUR20.4 a share on a gross basis. We then apply a roughly 38% holding-company discount because the value is not cleanly crystallizing: TKMS is already public and reflected in the price, the TK Elevator deal is 12-18 months away and about 75% in stock, Materials Services is a minority spin-off that stays consolidated, and central pension, restructuring, and corporate costs sit above the parts. That brings the base sum-of-the-parts value to about EUR12.50 a share.
risk_reward: Roughly one-to-one, modestly tilted to the upside. Upside to fair value of about EUR12.50 is +19% from EUR10.50. Downside to where support kicks in (net cash of EUR4.8 a share plus the listed TKMS, TK Elevator, and nucera stakes marked conservatively, an asset floor of about EUR8.5-9.0 a share) is -14% to -19%. The net-cash plus listed-stake floor caps the downside, but most of the gap between the share price and the value of the parts that would drive the upside has already been harvested by the roughly 240% run, so the payoff is no longer wide.
peer_comps_note: Against its peers, thyssenkrupp screens as rich on the operating metrics, and that holds up: it trades at a price of about 50 times free cash flow, with no positive margin of safety on any valuation method and a return on invested capital of -2.0%, versus European steel peers SSAB at about 6 times free cash flow, ArcelorMittal at about 5 times, and Outokumpu at about 4-5 times, all of which are structurally cheaper and earn positive returns. So on a like-for-like steel and operating basis, thyssenkrupp is expensive, not cheap. The case is not a bet on the steel multiple; it is a sum-of-the-parts / holding-company case where the value sits in net cash plus listed stakes (51% of TKMS, about 16% of TK Elevator) rather than in the operating earnings stream. Note that the roughly 50% EU steel re-rating over four months (on the carbon border levy, tariffs, and the emissions-trading delay) was a sector-wide move on a shared policy driver, not company-specific edge; the peers moved together on the same driver, so thyssenkrupp's steel improvement is sector beta, not unique advantage. In sum, the peer comparison confirms there is no operating-multiple discount left to harvest; any remaining edge is the asset backstop, and that backstop is now roughly fully priced (a EUR6.5bn market value against EUR3.0bn of net cash).
interrogator_dossier:
# A deep dive on management and the setup — thyssenkrupp AG (TKA.DE)

*Industrials, a diversified industrial holding company (steel, automotive technology, materials trading, decarbonization/hydrogen tech, 51% of marine/TKMS, about 16% of TK Elevator). Signal: deep value. Transcripts span the first quarter of fiscal 2024/25 (February 2025) through the first quarter of fiscal 2025/26 (February 2026).*

---

## 1. NARRATIVE ARC

The story across these six quarters is a **conglomerate self-liquidation dressed up as a green transformation**, and the emphasis shifts decisively over the window. Early calls (February 2025) lead with operations — the APEX 2.0 cost program, talk of resilience, a record marine order backlog (EUR16bn), low-carbon steel framework deals with Volkswagen, electrolyzer framework deals. By the December 2025 full-year call the lead has become explicitly **portfolio and structure**: management unveils ACES 2030, reframes the entire company as a future **financial holding company with strong independent entities** (a parent that owns and monetizes its businesses rather than running them), and stops pretending the operating businesses are the thesis. The throughline is *crystallizing value through separation*, with TKMS as the proof of concept.

What got **introduced**: the holding-company end-state (December 2025), the Jindal majority-sale track for steel (December 2025), the Materials Services capital-market-readiness track (February 2026), the automotive realignment into four business units plus the divestment of Automation Engineering (August 2025 through February 2026), and the HKM exit / Salzgitter term sheet ending captive slab supply in 2028 (February 2026). What got **executed**: the TKMS minority spin-off (49% distributed, 51% retained, a 1-for-20 ratio, listed in Frankfurt in October 2025, into the MDAX index within weeks); management claims over 14% value creation on the first day, on top of a roughly 240% rise in thyssenkrupp's shares in fiscal 2024/25.

The growth driver loudly introduced and now **quietly dropped** is the green-hydrogen story. The decarbonization unit (nucera electrolyzers, Polysius cement carbon-capture, Uhde ammonia) is presented every quarter as the future, yet by February 2026 it is the *worst* segment, with sales down 19% and operating profit falling EUR33m to -EUR16m on project deferrals. The framework deals (with VW, TITAN, Hydrom, Uniper) are non-binding and recur as talking points without converting to revenue. The real, repeating good news is narrow and concentrated: Marine Systems (now spun off) and the Bilstein aftermarket. Everything else is weak demand across most customer groups.

## 2. CLAIMS vs FINANCIALS (forensic)

| Management claim | Quarter(s) | The hard numbers say | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| "Increasing underlying resilience ... adjusted operating profit stable despite a roughly EUR5bn sales drop in 2 years" | repeated, especially December 2025 | Revenue went from 41.1B (fiscal 2022) to 37.5B to 35.0B to **32.8B (fiscal 2025)**, a 3-year compound decline of **-7.2%**. Adjusted operating profit did hold at about EUR640m in fiscal 2025 (+EUR72m year over year). | **Half-true.** Adjusted operating profit was genuinely defended through headcount cuts (-4,800 in fiscal 2025), but it sits atop a revenue base shrinking 6-7% a year. Defending a margin on a melting top line is managed decline, not resilience. |
| "Third consecutive year of positive free cash flow before M&A ... first time in 20 years" | every call | Fiscal 2025 free cash flow before M&A was **+EUR363m**, but flattered by a Marine prepayment of more than EUR1bn (cash in ahead of project cash out). **Trailing-twelve-month free cash flow is -EUR926m**; the reported price is about 50 times free cash flow; the free-cash-flow margin is 0.4%. | **Misleading framing.** The positive figure leans on advance customer cash that reverses; on a trailing basis the group burns cash. The first quarter of fiscal 2025/26 alone was -EUR1.5bn. |
| "Strong, largely unchanged ~EUR4bn net cash ... group basically debt-free" | February to August 2025 | Net cash went from EUR4.3B (February 2025) to EUR4.0B to EUR3.7B (August 2025) to about EUR5B (December 2025, after the India sale) to **EUR3.2B (February 2026)** to a recomputed **EUR3.03B net cash (2026-03-31)**. | **True but eroding.** A genuine net-cash balance sheet (a real add-back when valuing by the parts), but the trend is *down* once you strip out the India sale and Marine prepayment one-offs, and the first quarter burns EUR1.5bn seasonally. |
| Net income recovery | December 2025 / February 2026 | Net income went from -2.07B (fiscal 2023) to -1.51B (fiscal 2024) to **+EUR465m (fiscal 2025)**, but fiscal 2025 was positive only via a **+EUR902m Elevator revaluation** plus the India sale, *washing out* about EUR800m of steel write-downs. The first quarter of fiscal 2025/26 net income was **-EUR334m.** Return on equity -4.1%, return on invested capital -2.0%. | **Manufactured.** The single positive year is a revaluation-and-disposal artifact, not operating earnings. The underlying business remains loss-making; returns are *below zero*. |
| "APEX 2.0 ... significant measurable impact" | every call | The adjusted operating-profit defense is real (held flat on a EUR2.2bn fiscal 2025 sales drop). | **Credible — the one durable bright spot.** Cost execution is the part of the story the numbers actually back up. |
| Steel "hidden value," Jindal majority sale "on track" | December 2025 / February 2026 | Steel carries about EUR2.4bn of pension obligations plus mid-to-high three-figure-million-euro restructuring plus a EUR700-800m total restructuring guide; recurring write-downs of about EUR600m a year. | **Negative-value optics.** Steel is a *liability-laden* asset; the sale was about offloading a cash drain, not harvesting value, and it has since paused (see section 6). |

The decisive forensic point: **every positive headline number in the window traces to a one-off** (the Marine prepayment drove free cash flow; the Elevator revaluation plus the India sale drove net income; disposals drove net cash). The *recurring* operating engine produces a negative return on invested capital, -6% to -7% revenue, and a 1.4% net margin.

## 3. TONE & CONFIDENCE TRAJECTORY

**Trajectory: turning — stable-to-rising on STRATEGY, persistently defensive on OPERATIONS,** a widening split between confident portfolio language and apologetic demand language.

- **February 2025** (Lopez/Schulte): on the offensive about marine (record-breaking new orders, called a personal highlight), measured on markets (many industries remain muted, automotive under pressure). Schulte volunteers the famous tell on the call's last exchange, when analyst Fairclough notes: *"your net cash is over EUR4 billion. Your market cap is EUR3 billion ... you could just buy the whole company back, right?"* Lopez deflects: *"We are not doing this theoretical analysis right now."*
- **May 2025**: more defensive — *"markets remain weak overall,"* an auto cost cut of EUR150m plus the Hagen phase-out, *"down by 2,600 FTE."* The candid confession that the long-term incentive accrual took a hit of more than EUR30m *because* the share price ran is notable.
- **August 2025** (new CFO Hamann debuts): hedged — guidance specified to the lower end, sales down 9% in the quarter. Repeated *"cannot be excluded"* on further steel write-downs.
- **December 2025**: the most confident of the window, but the confidence is entirely **structural** — a year of decisions, a year of execution, ACES 2030, the TKMS blueprint, over 14% value creation. Operations are still under persistent market headwinds.
- **February 2026**: a genuinely new, *more bullish* note appears — steel sentiment turned positive, share prices up about 50%, the carbon border levy and tariffs framed as upside potential, the emissions-trading delay called another de-risking factor, a steel summit with Chancellor Merz and von der Leyen. This is the first quarter management talks *up* an operating business (steel) on outside policy tailwinds rather than purely on self-help.

The shift from offensive to defensive to structural-offensive is the signature of a **breakup story finding traction**: confidence is highest where the lever is corporate finance (separation) and lowest where it is end-market demand.

## 4. GUIDANCE CREDIBILITY

**Reliability: MEDIUM.** The earnings-beat rate is brutal — **1 of 6 quarters (17%)**, with earnings per share down at a 3-year compound rate of -25.6%, and on the *operating* line the pattern is serial top-line cuts (lowered the sales guidance in the first quarter; specified to the lower end in the third). So on demand and earnings per share, credibility is LOW and the numbers confirm it.

BUT credibility *splits by metric*: on **free cash flow before M&A**, management hit its guide three years running (even raising it once), and on **portfolio milestones** they delivered the headline acts — TKMS spun off on schedule (October 2025), the India electrical-steel business sold (about EUR440m, closed January 2025), the IG Metall restructuring agreed (December 2025), the bond repaid (debt-free). They **deliver on transactions and cash timing; they miss on operating demand.** There is no sandbagging pattern; if anything, the free-cash-flow guide is *honestly* flagged as one-off-flattered (they did not simply add the EUR1bn). Score the *deal-execution* track HIGH, the *operating-guidance* track LOW; blended to MEDIUM.

## 5. ANALYST PRESSURE POINTS

Analysts (Synagowitz and Bastian most persistently, plus Fairclough, Obst, and Gabriel) circle four unanswered things, and management deflects each:

1. **How much cash gets trapped in, or injected into, the steel carve-out?** Asked in August 2025 (is there a floor?) and answered with *"it will have the required liquidity"* — no number, repeatedly. This is the load-bearing unknown for valuing the parts, and they will not quantify it.
2. **The size of the Marine prepayment, and what is really in the cash balance.** Schulte (February 2025): *"we would not disclose any further figures ... it's above EUR1 billion."* The net-cash number is partly customer money.
3. **The pace of restructuring cash out.** Guided to EUR250m in fiscal 2025 plus a three-figure-million-euro fiscal 2026 steel figure plus an undefined auto figure; by February 2026 this escalated to **EUR700-800m total restructuring.** The cash drag keeps getting *bigger* each time they are pushed.
4. **Will further steel write-downs come?** Every quarter: *"cannot be excluded."* They never get to no.

The topic analysts pushed that *later turned into news*: the **steel-sale structure.** Synagowitz repeatedly probed (August 2025) whether a better policy backdrop could make thyssenkrupp *retain* steel; Lopez denied it ("I would not see any reason right now"). Four months later (February 2026) management itself conceded the positive sentiment "will have to be an input for the conversations with Jindal" — that is, the analyst's hypothesis (policy changes the steel calculus) was right, and it ultimately killed the Jindal deal (see section 6).

## 6. RED FLAGS / GREEN FLAGS

Red flag: **Trailing-twelve-month free cash flow is -EUR926m** despite the three-years-of-positive-free-cash-flow headline — the positive prints are advance-payment timing, not durable cash generation.
Red flag: **Every positive fiscal 2025 metric is a one-off** (the Elevator revaluation, the India sale, the Marine prepayment) washing out recurring write-downs and operating losses; return on equity -4.1%, return on invested capital -2.0%.
Red flag: **Revenue is in structural decline** (-6% to -7% a year, three years running) — resilience here means defending the margin while the base shrinks.
Red flag: **Recurring steel write-downs of about EUR600m a year** plus EUR2.4bn of steel pension obligations plus the EUR700-800m restructuring guide — steel is a value *destroyer*, and the sale was about shedding liabilities.
Red flag: **The deep-value anchor has evaporated.** The February 2025 gap of net cash above EUR4bn against a market value of EUR3bn that defined the thesis is *gone* after the roughly 240% fiscal 2025 run — market value is now EUR6.5bn against about EUR3.0bn of net cash. The cheapness the screener flagged is **stale.**
Green flag: **Cost execution (APEX 2.0) is real and corroborated** — adjusted operating profit held flat across a EUR2.2bn fiscal 2025 revenue drop; the 4,800 headcount cut was delivered.
Green flag: **Transaction-delivery credibility is high** — TKMS spun off on time, India sold, the bond repaid, restructuring agreed. The breakup machine works.
Green flag: **A genuine net-cash balance sheet** (EUR3.0bn at 2026-03-31, cash of EUR3.84bn against total debt of EUR0.81bn) — a legitimate add-back when valuing the parts, and a downside cushion.
Green flag: **The EU steel policy tailwind is a new, outside positive** (a tighter carbon border levy, import quotas, an emissions-trading delay) that re-rated European steel about 50% in four months and ultimately let thyssenkrupp keep steel on better terms.

## 7. HIDDEN SIGNALS

- **The CFO change mid-window** (Schulte handing to Axel Hamann, who debuted in August 2025). Hamann frames things in much more transactional, holding-company terms — he is the voice of tk accelis, capital-market readiness, materials-as-a-service, and the deferred-tax-asset write-down mechanics. The new CFO is a *breakup CFO*, signaling that the holding-company pivot is structural, not rhetorical.
- **The terminology migrates**: segments become stand-alone businesses open to third parties become a financial holding company. Each step is management telling you the operating businesses will be *sold or floated*, not fixed.
- **The "we could buy the whole company back" exchange (February 2025) is the thesis's birth and death certificate** — it was true at a EUR3bn market value; the roughly 240% run has since closed the very arbitrage the deep-value signal is pricing.
- **Marine Systems has dropped out of the consolidated growth narrative** — once the crown jewel (a personal highlight), it is now a 51%-held, separately listed entity; the *parent's* remaining ongoing businesses (steel, auto, materials, decarbonization) are the lower-quality remainder.
- **"Capital market readiness" is a euphemism for "not yet sellable"** — applied to Materials Services and implicitly to auto; management is openly staging which subsidiaries are dressed for exit.

## 8. CAPITAL-ALLOCATION VERDICT (for the director)

**Classification: turning** — decisively, and competently, from an operating industrial into a financial holding company that serially separates its businesses. This is a genuine self-help breakup executed by a credible deal team, not a drift.

**The single most important thing the director must weigh before sizing:** *the deep-value entry has already fired.* The thesis the screener tagged — buy a net-cash conglomerate below the value of its parts — was live at the EUR3bn market value of early 2025; after a roughly 240% run to a EUR6.5bn market value (now well *above* net cash), the gap between the price and the value of the parts has largely closed, and the marquee catalyst (the TKMS spin-off) is **spent.** What remains is (a) a *minority* Materials Services spin-off (thyssenkrupp keeps control, so it is modest crystallization, not a clean unlock) and (b) an Elevator/Kone deal that is real but slow (12-18 months of competition review). The director is no longer underwriting a wide discount to the parts; she is underwriting *residual* crystallization on a loss-making, cash-burning operating core after most of the easy value has been harvested. Size to the *remaining* gap, not the historical one.

**ECONOMIC MOAT (a durable competitive advantage).** The advantage here would be scale-cost in commodity steel plus engineering know-how in marine and components — but none of it produces excess returns. The evidence is dispositive: return on invested capital -2.0%, return on equity -4.1%, net margin 1.4%, gross margin 11.3%, three years of recurring write-downs. A business earning *below zero* on its capital across a full cycle has **no durable advantage** in the economic sense — there is no excess-return base to protect. The cleanest franchise (marine/TKMS) has been spun out of the parent. Competitive advantage: **none**; trend: **eroding** (margins and returns are negative and the captive-supply structure is unwinding — HKM slab supply ends 2028).

**SECULAR THREAT.** The European steel and combustion-engine auto-components core sits squarely in the energy-transition-loser lane: decarbonization mandates impose enormous green-steel capital spending (a direct-reduced-iron plant in Duisburg) on a structurally low-return commodity, while the auto-components book (springs, stabilizers, steering) is tied to a shrinking combustion-engine supply chain. This threat is **material, not terminal** — partly because the same energy-transition pressure cuts *both* ways here (the carbon border levy, tariffs, and the emissions-trading delay are currently *protecting* EU steel, the rare case where the loser theme generates a near-term policy tailwind), and partly because the net-cash balance sheet buys time. But the long-run economics of the operating core are a structurally shrinking, below-cost-of-capital base. The cheapness is therefore a **mixed picture**: the *balance sheet* (net cash plus the Elevator stake plus remaining spin-off optionality) is a genuine asset floor, but the *operating earnings base* is a value trap. The stock is now priced for the breakup to keep delivering — most of which it already has.

Credibility: 3 of 5. Trajectory: turning. Competitive advantage: none, and eroding. Secular threat: material.
